text.title: Agency Case Study Template Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of `Agency Case Study Template` must enforce. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: The case study MUST follow the five-part structure: Context (industry, size, role), Problem (pain + cost), Approach (what the agency did, in 3-5 bullets), Outcome (≥ 1 quantified metric), Lessons (what would change next time). Sections cannot be merged or dropped.
rule.rationale: Free-form case studies bury the outcome; the five-part structure forces the metric and lesson into reader's view within 60 seconds.

rule.statement: The Outcome section MUST contain ≥ 1 quantified metric (% lift, $ saved, hours saved, NPS shift) with baseline + post + source link. Hand-wave outcomes are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Un-quantified case studies convert at 5-10% vs quantified at 25-40%; the metric is the proof asset.

rule.statement: The case study MUST contain ≥ 1 verbatim client quote (with named role or anonymised role) signed off by the client. Paraphrased quotes are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Paraphrased quotes read as ghostwritten; verbatim quotes (even short) carry the trust signal that converts skeptical inbound.

rule.statement: Each study MUST name an internal approver (founder or senior contributor) and an external approver (client). Both must sign off before publish.
rule.rationale: Un-approved studies often misrepresent client work, leading to legal letters; dual sign-off is the safety mechanism.

rule.statement: Each quantitative claim MUST link to a source (analytics dashboard screenshot, contract metric, client email, public stat). Un-anchored claims are demoted to qualitative language.
rule.rationale: Un-anchored claims invite challenge from prospects; the evidence link forces honesty + builds credibility.

rule.statement: When preconditions in `Applies If` fail OR any item in `Skip If` triggers, do not run this methodology. Document the skip rationale in the artefact's parent log.
rule.rationale: Misapplied methodologies are worse than no methodology — they consume review cycles and produce false confidence.
